narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at past students' profiles—like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans—to predict if they'll pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 77% chance that you would not, which is why your application was denied to protect the school's outcomes. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—far below the typical student's middle-range standing—along with a middle family income bracket, an undergraduate GPA of 2.10 (compared to the average 3.21), an LSAT score of 30 (below the usual 36.7), and plans to attend full-time like most applicants. The two biggest factors pushing the prediction toward bar failure were your low third-year school decile and your low first-year decile, as these early academic struggles stood out strongly against students who typically rank higher and go on to pass the bar. Your undergraduate GPA actually helped a bit, pulling the prediction slightly toward passing since it's a key strength relative to some, while your LSAT nudged it in the same positive direction, though not enough to offset the rest. Your middle family income contributed modestly toward the failure prediction compared to higher-income peers who often have more resources, and your full-time status had almost no impact since it's the norm. Overall, your grades in school deciles were the most influential red flags, placing you well below the profiles of students predicted to succeed. This doesn't define your potential—many overcome similar starts through targeted improvement. Next, I recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking academic advising to improve your decile rankings if possible, and building a stronger narrative in your application about your resilience as a Black woman in law. Consider community colleges or prep programs tailored for underrepresented students to strengthen your record. You have the drive; focus on these steps, reapply stronger, and keep pushing forward—success stories like yours are out there waiting.
